concerns.analysis: Several meaningful images are broken or missing. The image manifest indicates that the base64 data for many images (such as the cover photographs on page 1 and the site overview/vegetation photos on pages 23-28) is corrupt ('valid': false). Additionally, the maps on pages 13, 14, 15, and 20 (e.g., 'Figure 1. Project Vicinity Map') are missing `` tags entirely in the HTML and have been replaced with text placeholders.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Page 1 contains a photograph of a vegetative buffer strip. Page 13 contains a map titled 'Figure 1. Project Vicinity Map'.
concerns.output_quote: The image data for the page 1 photo is corrupted according to the manifest. The map on page 13 lacks an `` tag and is replaced by `[Image: A project vicinity map for the Running Dog Mitigation Site...]`.

concerns.description: Fidelity Content evaluation failed — dimension not assessed, requires human review
concerns.analysis: The content fidelity call did not produce valid results.

concerns.description: Fidelity Structural evaluation failed — dimension not assessed, requires human review
concerns.analysis: The structural fidelity call did not produce valid results.

concerns.description: Table split across page break(s): 5 table(s) with repeated headers across consecutive pages
concerns.analysis: split_table_count=5. Examples: pages 34-35: 7 cols (plot, scientific name, common name...); pages 35-36: 7 cols (plot, scientific name, common name...); pages 36-37: 7 cols (plot, scientific name, common name...). Tables spanning multiple pages were extracted as separate table elements with duplicated header rows. The HTML output will show multiple tables instead of one continuous table — a structural accessibility issue.

concerns.description: Table caption coded as header row: 3 table(s) have a single spanning first row that is likely a caption/title
concerns.analysis: caption_as_header=3. Examples: p16: "TOTAL AREA OF BUFFER MITIGATION (TABM)"; p18: "Project Information"; p33: "Vegetation Performance Standards Summary Table". First row spans all columns — should be a <caption> element, not a header row.

concerns.description:
Header/footer elements retained in output (26): Wildlands Engineering, Inc. • phone 704-332-7754 • fax 704-3; Wildlands Engineering, Inc. • phone 704-332-7754 • fax 704-3; Running Dog Mitigation Site
Monitoring Year 1 Annual Report
concerns.analysis: header_footer_count=26. Header/footer text from extraction JSON is present in the rendered HTML — the renderer failed to strip it. This is a structural accessibility issue.

concerns.description: 8 bare URL(s) in text not converted to hyperlinks: http://cvs.bio.unc.edu/protocol/cvs-eep-protocol-v4.2-lev1-2.pdf; https://files.nc.gov/ncdeq/Mitigation%20Services/Watershed_Planning/Yadkin_River; (paul.wiesner@ncdenr.gov; <katie.merritt@ncdenr.gov; <Kelly.Phillips@deq.nc.gov
concerns.analysis: bare_url_count=8. URLs appear as plain text instead of clickable links.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 5 table(s) have a data row marked as header: "Appendix 1", "Yadkin - Goose Creek", "Activity or Report"
concerns.analysis: data_in_th=0, datarow_as_header=5. Tables use <th> for data values (misrepresenting data as headers) or have a data row mismarked as a header row. Screen readers rely on <th> to announce column/row context.

concerns.description: Repeated footer/header text (2x): "1430 s. mint st, suite 104"
concerns.analysis: Short text with footer-like pattern appears on 2 pages

concerns.description: Displaced hyperlinks: 2 link(s) extracted as standalone elements instead of inline text
concerns.analysis: standalone_links=2, examples=['mailto:aeckardt@wildlandseng.com', 'mailto:aeckardt@wildlandseng.com']. Links should be inline within paragraphs, not separate blocks.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 7 table(s) with 3+ rows have no <th> header cells
concerns.analysis: data_in_th_time=0, no_th_tables=7. Minor semantic markup issues: tables lack <th> elements or use time values as headers. These affect screen reader context but are less severe than data misrepresentation.
